title: Carved walnut coffee spoon
subtitle: A small seasoned-wood spoon for espresso and fine salt
excerpt: A small, elegant coffee spoon in seasoned walnut: 14 cm total length, narrow oval bowl, handle that tapers to a point. Walnut is the darkest and densest of the common spoon woods; it takes a fine surface and pairs well with ceramics. A short project for an afternoon at the bench.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for small treen spoon proportions; traditional spoon-carving convention for the hook-knife hollow on a narrow bowl.

glossaryTerms.definition: Air-dried or kiln-dried timber stabilised at 8 to 12 percent moisture content for indoor use.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the Swedish school-craft standard for whittling and spoon-carving.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
The slot a saw cut leaves; in carving, a cut that is open enough to be visible and serves as a boundary marker.

content.text: A coffee spoon is the smallest finished piece in the spoon-carving sub-category and the one that rewards careful surface work most visibly. The blank is thin, the bowl narrow, and the handle tapers to a clean point. Walnut carves well in
seasoned wood

; no green-wood period, no drying time; and the dark colour makes a striking pair with white or cream ceramics. Note the iron-reactivity: walnut reacts with iron tools to produce a dark surface stain. Wipe the finished spoon with a cloth dampened with a weak citric acid solution (a teaspoon in 250 ml of water) after carving to remove any iron-tannin staining before oiling.
The wood

A piece of air-dried or kiln-dried walnut: 16 cm long, 4 cm wide, 1.5 cm thick. Walnut off-cuts from a joinery or furniture project are ideal. The grain should run straight along the length of the blank; a blank with wavy or interlocked grain will be difficult to hollow with the small hook knife. Cherry is an alternative with similar properties and less iron reactivity.
Tools
A
sloyd knife

and a small hook knife. A leather strop. Board butter for finishing. Because the blank is small, every cut is close to the fingers; four-finger grip on the blank and thumb-push grip on the knife are especially important here. No cut whose blade path can reach a finger.
Method
Use
push cuts
throughout. The blank is small enough that every cut is precise; resist the temptation to rush. Strop every 10 minutes of work; walnut dulls edges faster than sycamore.
Draw the spoon outline on the flat face: a 10 cm handle tapering to a 5 mm tip, a 4 cm narrow oval bowl. Saw a small
kerf
at the neck boundary; this sets the transition between handle and bowl and stops the grain tearing across the neck during hollowing.
Whittle the waste from the handle outline with push cuts. The handle tapers: 1 cm wide at the neck, 5 mm at the tip. The cross-section is a flattened oval, thicker on the palm side.

Hollow the bowl with the small hook knife in short arcs; 2 cm at most. The bowl depth is 6 mm at the centre. Walnut grain typically runs straight so the arcs should cut cleanly in both directions; if one direction tears, cut only in the other.
Shape the bowl back with push cuts on the sloyd knife. Bowl wall: 2 to 3 mm at the rim, 3 mm at the floor. The bowl back is convex, not flat.
If iron staining is visible (a dark discolouration on the cut surfaces), wipe down with a citric acid solution; one teaspoon in 250 ml of water; and leave to dry before sanding.
Sand through 120, 180, and 240 grit. Walnut responds well to fine sanding and the surface at 240 grit is close to what a hand plane leaves.
Finishing
Apply board butter to all surfaces, leave 15 minutes, buff off excess. Board butter re-coats easily; apply before each period of regular use. Walnut with board butter is food-safe and stable.
Variations
Cherry: warmer colour, less iron reactivity. Use the same dimensions and method.
Salt spoon: reduce to 10 cm total length and 3 cm bowl. The smallest useful treen spoon.
Care
Wash and dry promptly. Re-coat with board butter monthly. Walnut darkens and improves with use.

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
attrs.intro: Common problems with the walnut coffee spoon.

items.cause: Iron-tannin reaction between walnut and the steel blade
items.fix: Wipe with a weak citric acid solution before sanding. The stain is surface-only and sands out at 120 grit if caught early.

items.symptom: Hook knife tears the bowl rather than cutting
items.cause: Edge too dull for walnut's density, or cutting against the grain
items.fix: Strop the hook knife and try a shorter arc. If tearing persists in one direction, reverse the arc direction.

items.cause: Tip too thin before the neck boundary was fully set
items.fix: Increase the handle tip width to 7 mm. A 5 mm tip is the minimum for walnut; thinner is fragile.
